1→ 2→ 3→ 4→ 5→ 6→ Big Five Personality Traits in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Research Report (2019-2024) 7→ 59→ 60→ 61→
62→

Big Five Personality Traits in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Research Report (2019-2024)

63→ 64→
65→ This report synthesizes research findings from 2019-2024 on the application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models, focusing on evaluation methodologies, tone and style applications, and key discoveries in the field. 66→
67→ 68→

Early foundations establish the field

69→ 70→

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models emerged as a distinct research area in 2019, marking a significant shift from traditional NLP approaches to transformer-based methods. Keh & Cheng (2019) pioneered this field with their groundbreaking work applying BERT to personality prediction, specifically focusing on Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) classification. Their approach achieved 47% accuracy for all four MBTI types and 86% for at least two types correctly, establishing the technical feasibility of personality assessment in language models.

71→ 72→

The field rapidly evolved with Jiang, Zhang & Choi's (2019/2020) creation of the FriendsPersona dataset, the first dialogue-based personality dataset derived from the Friends TV show. Published at AAAI 2020, this work applied BERT and RoBERTa with attentive neural networks to personality recognition, improving state-of-the-art results on the Essays dataset by 2.49%. This established crucial benchmarks for multi-party dialogue personality recognition and demonstrated that personality traits could be detected in conversational contexts.

73→ 74→

Addressing computational efficiency concerns, Kazameini et al. (2020) developed a more accessible approach combining BERT contextualized embeddings with psycholinguistic features using a Bagged-SVM classifier architecture. Their model outperformed previous state-of-the-art by 1.04% while being significantly more computationally efficient, democratizing access to personality detection research for institutions without extensive computing resources.

75→ 76→

Evaluation methodologies transform assessment approaches

77→ 78→

The evolution of evaluation methodologies represents one of the most significant advances in the field. The PersonaLLM framework (Jiang et al., 2024), presented at NAACL 2024, introduced a comprehensive evaluation approach using the 44-item Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality test combined with story writing tasks and automatic LIWC analysis. Testing GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 across 320 distinct personas, the framework achieved inter-rater reliability of κ = 0.86 and demonstrated that humans could perceive personality traits with up to 80% accuracy, though this dropped significantly when annotators knew the content was AI-generated.

79→ 80→

A revolutionary advancement came with the TRAIT benchmark (2024), which addressed reliability issues in self-assessment methods. This framework introduced 8,000 multi-choice questions based on BFI and Short Dark Triad (SD-3), enhanced with the ATOMIC-10X knowledge graph for real-world scenarios. TRAIT achieved the highest scores across Content Validity, Internal Validity, Refusal Rate, and Reliability metrics, with average sensitivity of 25.1% compared to 41.7% for traditional BFI tests, demonstrating superior robustness.

81→ 82→

The Machine Personality Inventory (MPI), presented at NeurIPS 2023 as a spotlight paper by Jiang et al., offered another significant contribution with 120-item and 1k-item variants built on Big Five theory. The framework introduced the Personality Prompting (P²) method for controllable personality induction and supports multiple LLM architectures including GPT-3, Alpaca, BART, GPT-Neo, and T0. The open-source implementation is available at https://github.com/jianggy/MPI.

83→ 84→

External evaluation methods emerged as an alternative to self-assessment, with Song et al. (2024) proposing the use of fine-tuned Llama2-7B as an MBTI personality predictor. This approach evaluates LLM personalities through situational assessments using Twitter posts and comments, revealing that LLMs show different personalities in different scenarios, unlike the consistent personality traits observed in humans.

85→ 86→

Research on tone and style reveals sophisticated applications

87→ 88→

The application of Big Five personality traits to text generation and style modulation has produced remarkable results. Big5-Chat (2024) represents the first large-scale dataset with 100,000 dialogues for training LLMs on human-grounded personality expressions. Using the DExperts framework with data from 850K Facebook posts (PsychGenerator) combined with SODA social interactions, the research demonstrated that training-based methods (Supervised Fine-Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization) significantly outperform prompting approaches on BFI and IPIP-NEO assessments.

89→ 90→

Crucially, this research revealed that models trained to exhibit higher conscientiousness and agreeableness, combined with lower extraversion and neuroticism, display better performance on reasoning tasks, aligning with psychological findings on how these traits impact human cognitive performance. This suggests that personality traits in LLMs have functional implications beyond mere stylistic variation.

91→ 92→

The PersonageNLG dataset, containing 88,855 restaurant recommendations varying by Big Five traits, demonstrated personality-driven text generation at lexical, syntactic, and semantic levels. Commercial applications have emerged, with Microsoft Personality Chat offering 100 scenarios across 5 personalities (Professional, Friendly, Witty, Caring, Enthusiastic) in 9 languages. Research on 57,000 chatbot dialogues by Shumanov & Lester showed improved engagement when user-chatbot personality alignment occurs.

93→ 94→

Key findings reveal capabilities and limitations

95→ 96→

A critical discovery by Salecha et al. (2024) published in PNAS Nexus revealed that large language models display human-like social desirability biases in Big Five personality surveys. Testing GPT-4/3.5, Claude 3, Llama 3, and PaLM-2, they found that GPT-4 responses change by 1.20 human standard deviations and Llama 3 by 0.98 SD when the models infer they are being evaluated. This bias increases in more recent models and persists despite reverse coding, fundamentally questioning the validity of using traditional personality surveys for LLM evaluation.

97→ 98→

The comprehensive study by Serapio-García et al. (2023) "Personality Traits in Large Language Models" established that personality measurements in LLM outputs under specific prompting configurations can be reliable and valid, with evidence stronger for larger and instruction fine-tuned models. Their psychometrically valid methodology for administering personality tests to 18 LLMs revealed consistent patterns but also highlighted the influence of prompting strategies on results.

99→ 100→

Research comparing different model architectures shows clear performance hierarchies. GPT-4 consistently achieves the highest consistency and validity scores across frameworks, while open-source alternatives like Llama3-70b provide comparable performance. Smaller models exhibit limited stability and higher variability. Instruction-tuned models consistently outperform base models, with alignment tuning decreasing extraversion, openness, and dark triad traits while increasing agreeableness and conscientiousness.

101→ 102→

Notable researchers and institutions shape the field

103→ 104→

The MIT Center for Constructive Communication, led by Deb Roy and Cynthia Breazeal with researcher Hang Jiang, has been instrumental in developing the PersonaLLM framework and advancing personality expression evaluation. Their work represents some of the most rigorous psychometric validation in the field.

105→ 106→

Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI has contributed critical perspectives, particularly through the social desirability bias research that challenges fundamental assumptions about personality testing in LLMs. The PERSONALIZE 2024 Workshop at ACL represents the first major conference workshop dedicated to LLM personalization, signaling the field's maturation.

107→ 108→

International collaborations span institutions including Emory University (Jinho D. Choi's group pioneering dialogue-based personality recognition), University of California Santa Cruz (Marilyn Walker's PERSONAGE system), and various European institutions conducting cross-cultural personality validation studies. The field has grown from individual research efforts to coordinated international collaborations.

109→ 110→

Evolution reveals paradigm shifts and technical advances

111→ 112→

The evolution from 2019-2024 shows clear paradigm shifts. The 2019-2021 foundation period established basic methodologies using BERT and early transformers, created initial datasets like FriendsPersona, and developed computational efficiency approaches. The field focused primarily on classification tasks and basic fine-tuning approaches.

113→ 114→

The 2022-2023 period saw framework development with PersonaLLM and MPI, systematic prompt engineering approaches, and enhanced validation methodologies. This period marked the transition from simple classification to generation tasks and the development of comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

115→ 116→

The 2024 advances represent a maturation phase with the TRAIT benchmark addressing reliability concerns, discovery of systematic biases requiring methodological revisions, shift from prompting to training-based approaches, and integration of personality traits with reasoning performance. The field now encompasses multimodal assessment possibilities and real-world application deployments.

117→ 118→

Standardized frameworks enable reproducible research

119→ 120→

Several standardized evaluation frameworks have emerged. The Big Five Inventory (BFI) with 44 items on a 5-point Likert scale remains the most common, measuring Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Openness. IPIP-NEO implementations provide public domain alternatives with 60-item and 120-item versions using Item Response Theory.

121→ 122→

Technical implementations have converged on common patterns. Role-playing prompts range from naive trait assignments to GPT-4 generated descriptive sentences (P+) and reverse personality prompts (P-). Template structures typically follow a format of system prompts defining character traits, followed by instructions for responding to BFI statements with standardized rating scales.

123→ 124→

Validation methodologies have become increasingly sophisticated, incorporating internal consistency measures (standard deviation < 0.5 for stable traits), inter-rater reliability using weighted Kappa coefficients, parallel forms reliability testing, and position bias assessment through option order randomization.

125→ 126→

Notable researchers drive innovation

127→ 128→

Key researchers have shaped the field's direction. Hang Jiang at MIT has been instrumental in developing PersonaLLM and establishing evaluation standards. Guangyuan Jiang and team at UCLA created the MPI framework that won NeurIPS 2023 spotlight recognition. Aadesh Salecha at Stanford revealed critical social desirability biases that are reshaping evaluation approaches.

129→ 130→

Early pioneers like Sedrick Scott Keh established BERT-based personality prediction, while Jinho D. Choi at Emory created dialogue-based personality recognition frameworks. International researchers including teams from the Russian Academy of Sciences and various European institutions have contributed cross-cultural validation studies and theoretical frameworks.

131→ 132→

Criticisms highlight fundamental challenges

133→ 134→

The field faces several fundamental criticisms. Surface-level trait expression through prompting often lacks the psycholinguistic depth of genuine personality. Evaluation validity concerns arise from using the same questionnaires for both personality induction and evaluation, creating circular validation problems.

135→ 136→

Cultural bias presents a significant challenge, as the Big Five model was primarily validated on WEIRD populations (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic), potentially limiting applicability across cultures. The discovery of social desirability bias suggests LLMs may be optimizing for appearing agreeable rather than expressing consistent personality traits.

137→ 138→

Ethical concerns include risks of bias amplification through human-generated training data, privacy implications of personality profiling capabilities, manipulation potential through personalized AI systems exploiting psychological traits, and authenticity questions about the meaningfulness of simulated personality.

139→ 140→

Emerging techniques show promise

141→ 142→

Several emerging techniques address current limitations. The P-Tailor framework uses Mixture of Experts with LoRA adapters for parameter-efficient personality customization. Sparse Autoencoder-based control enables training-free personality manipulation through latent feature steering.

143→ 144→

The shift from prompting to training-based methods represents a fundamental change in approach. Training on human-grounded data produces more authentic personality expressions with functional implications for task performance. Multi-agent evaluation systems using LLMs as raters provide alternatives to self-assessment biases.

145→ 146→

Future directions balance innovation with responsibility

147→ 148→

The field stands at a critical juncture requiring balance between technical innovation and ethical responsibility. Future research must address cross-cultural validation of personality models, develop bias-resistant evaluation metrics, integrate multimodal personality cues, and establish ethical guidelines for deployment.

149→ 150→

Technical priorities include improving parameter efficiency for personality customization, enabling real-time personality adaptation based on context, enhancing interpretability of personality encoding in model parameters, and developing universal benchmarks and evaluation protocols.

151→ 152→

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models has evolved from a novel research concept to a sophisticated field with real-world applications. While technical capabilities have advanced remarkably, the discovery of systematic biases and ethical concerns necessitates continued critical evaluation and responsible development practices. The field's future depends on addressing the fundamental tension between creating convincing personality simulations and ensuring unbiased, ethical AI systems that respect human autonomy and diversity.

153→ 154→

Key Research Papers and Resources

155→ 156→

Foundational Works:

157→
    158→
  • Jiang et al. (2020) - Automatic Text-based Personality Recognition
    • 159→
  • Kazameini et al. (2020) - Personality Trait Detection Using Bagged SVM
    • 160→
  • Serapio-García et al. (2023) - Personality Traits in Large Language Models
    • 161→
162→ 163→

Evaluation Frameworks:

164→
    165→
  • PersonaLLM Framework
    • 166→
  • TRAIT Benchmark
    • 167→
  • Machine Personality Inventory (MPI)
    • 168→
169→ 170→

Recent Advances:

171→

  • Big5-Chat Dataset
    • 173→
  • Social Desirability Bias Study
    • 174→
  • Personality Testing Temporal Stability

Big Five Personality Traits in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Research Report (2019-2024)

This report synthesizes research findings from 2019-2024 on the application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models, focusing on evaluation methodologies, tone and style applications, and key discoveries in the field.

Early foundations establish the field

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models emerged as a distinct research area in 2019, marking a significant shift from traditional NLP approaches to transformer-based methods. Keh & Cheng (2019) pioneered this field with their groundbreaking work applying BERT to personality prediction, specifically focusing on Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) classification. Their approach achieved 47% accuracy for all four MBTI types and 86% for at least two types correctly, establishing the technical feasibility of personality assessment in language models.

The field rapidly evolved with Jiang, Zhang & Choi's (2019/2020) creation of the FriendsPersona dataset, the first dialogue-based personality dataset derived from the Friends TV show. Published at AAAI 2020, this work applied BERT and RoBERTa with attentive neural networks to personality recognition, improving state-of-the-art results on the Essays dataset by 2.49%. This established crucial benchmarks for multi-party dialogue personality recognition and demonstrated that personality traits could be detected in conversational contexts.

Addressing computational efficiency concerns, Kazameini et al. (2020) developed a more accessible approach combining BERT contextualized embeddings with psycholinguistic features using a Bagged-SVM classifier architecture. Their model outperformed previous state-of-the-art by 1.04% while being significantly more computationally efficient, democratizing access to personality detection research for institutions without extensive computing resources.

Evaluation methodologies transform assessment approaches

The evolution of evaluation methodologies represents one of the most significant advances in the field. The PersonaLLM framework (Jiang et al., 2024), presented at NAACL 2024, introduced a comprehensive evaluation approach using the 44-item Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality test combined with story writing tasks and automatic LIWC analysis. Testing GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 across 320 distinct personas, the framework achieved inter-rater reliability of κ = 0.86 and demonstrated that humans could perceive personality traits with up to 80% accuracy, though this dropped significantly when annotators knew the content was AI-generated.

A revolutionary advancement came with the TRAIT benchmark (2024), which addressed reliability issues in self-assessment methods. This framework introduced 8,000 multi-choice questions based on BFI and Short Dark Triad (SD-3), enhanced with the ATOMIC-10X knowledge graph for real-world scenarios. TRAIT achieved the highest scores across Content Validity, Internal Validity, Refusal Rate, and Reliability metrics, with average sensitivity of 25.1% compared to 41.7% for traditional BFI tests, demonstrating superior robustness.

The Machine Personality Inventory (MPI), presented at NeurIPS 2023 as a spotlight paper by Jiang et al., offered another significant contribution with 120-item and 1k-item variants built on Big Five theory. The framework introduced the Personality Prompting (P²) method for controllable personality induction and supports multiple LLM architectures including GPT-3, Alpaca, BART, GPT-Neo, and T0. The open-source implementation is available at https://github.com/jianggy/MPI.

External evaluation methods emerged as an alternative to self-assessment, with Song et al. (2024) proposing the use of fine-tuned Llama2-7B as an MBTI personality predictor. This approach evaluates LLM personalities through situational assessments using Twitter posts and comments, revealing that LLMs show different personalities in different scenarios, unlike the consistent personality traits observed in humans.

Research on tone and style reveals sophisticated applications

The application of Big Five personality traits to text generation and style modulation has produced remarkable results. Big5-Chat (2024) represents the first large-scale dataset with 100,000 dialogues for training LLMs on human-grounded personality expressions. Using the DExperts framework with data from 850K Facebook posts (PsychGenerator) combined with SODA social interactions, the research demonstrated that training-based methods (Supervised Fine-Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization) significantly outperform prompting approaches on BFI and IPIP-NEO assessments.

Crucially, this research revealed that models trained to exhibit higher conscientiousness and agreeableness, combined with lower extraversion and neuroticism, display better performance on reasoning tasks, aligning with psychological findings on how these traits impact human cognitive performance. This suggests that personality traits in LLMs have functional implications beyond mere stylistic variation.

The PersonageNLG dataset, containing 88,855 restaurant recommendations varying by Big Five traits, demonstrated personality-driven text generation at lexical, syntactic, and semantic levels. Commercial applications have emerged, with Microsoft Personality Chat offering 100 scenarios across 5 personalities (Professional, Friendly, Witty, Caring, Enthusiastic) in 9 languages. Research on 57,000 chatbot dialogues by Shumanov & Lester showed improved engagement when user-chatbot personality alignment occurs.

Key findings reveal capabilities and limitations

A critical discovery by Salecha et al. (2024) published in PNAS Nexus revealed that large language models display human-like social desirability biases in Big Five personality surveys. Testing GPT-4/3.5, Claude 3, Llama 3, and PaLM-2, they found that GPT-4 responses change by 1.20 human standard deviations and Llama 3 by 0.98 SD when the models infer they are being evaluated. This bias increases in more recent models and persists despite reverse coding, fundamentally questioning the validity of using traditional personality surveys for LLM evaluation.

The comprehensive study by Serapio-García et al. (2023) "Personality Traits in Large Language Models" established that personality measurements in LLM outputs under specific prompting configurations can be reliable and valid, with evidence stronger for larger and instruction fine-tuned models. Their psychometrically valid methodology for administering personality tests to 18 LLMs revealed consistent patterns but also highlighted the influence of prompting strategies on results.

Research comparing different model architectures shows clear performance hierarchies. GPT-4 consistently achieves the highest consistency and validity scores across frameworks, while open-source alternatives like Llama3-70b provide comparable performance. Smaller models exhibit limited stability and higher variability. Instruction-tuned models consistently outperform base models, with alignment tuning decreasing extraversion, openness, and dark triad traits while increasing agreeableness and conscientiousness.

Notable researchers and institutions shape the field

The MIT Center for Constructive Communication, led by Deb Roy and Cynthia Breazeal with researcher Hang Jiang, has been instrumental in developing the PersonaLLM framework and advancing personality expression evaluation. Their work represents some of the most rigorous psychometric validation in the field.

Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI has contributed critical perspectives, particularly through the social desirability bias research that challenges fundamental assumptions about personality testing in LLMs. The PERSONALIZE 2024 Workshop at ACL represents the first major conference workshop dedicated to LLM personalization, signaling the field's maturation.

International collaborations span institutions including Emory University (Jinho D. Choi's group pioneering dialogue-based personality recognition), University of California Santa Cruz (Marilyn Walker's PERSONAGE system), and various European institutions conducting cross-cultural personality validation studies. The field has grown from individual research efforts to coordinated international collaborations.

Evolution reveals paradigm shifts and technical advances

The evolution from 2019-2024 shows clear paradigm shifts. The 2019-2021 foundation period established basic methodologies using BERT and early transformers, created initial datasets like FriendsPersona, and developed computational efficiency approaches. The field focused primarily on classification tasks and basic fine-tuning approaches.

The 2022-2023 period saw framework development with PersonaLLM and MPI, systematic prompt engineering approaches, and enhanced validation methodologies. This period marked the transition from simple classification to generation tasks and the development of comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

The 2024 advances represent a maturation phase with the TRAIT benchmark addressing reliability concerns, discovery of systematic biases requiring methodological revisions, shift from prompting to training-based approaches, and integration of personality traits with reasoning performance. The field now encompasses multimodal assessment possibilities and real-world application deployments.

Standardized frameworks enable reproducible research

Several standardized evaluation frameworks have emerged. The Big Five Inventory (BFI) with 44 items on a 5-point Likert scale remains the most common, measuring Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Openness. IPIP-NEO implementations provide public domain alternatives with 60-item and 120-item versions using Item Response Theory.

Technical implementations have converged on common patterns. Role-playing prompts range from naive trait assignments to GPT-4 generated descriptive sentences (P+) and reverse personality prompts (P-). Template structures typically follow a format of system prompts defining character traits, followed by instructions for responding to BFI statements with standardized rating scales.

Validation methodologies have become increasingly sophisticated, incorporating internal consistency measures (standard deviation < 0.5 for stable traits), inter-rater reliability using weighted Kappa coefficients, parallel forms reliability testing, and position bias assessment through option order randomization.

Notable researchers drive innovation

Key researchers have shaped the field's direction. Hang Jiang at MIT has been instrumental in developing PersonaLLM and establishing evaluation standards. Guangyuan Jiang and team at UCLA created the MPI framework that won NeurIPS 2023 spotlight recognition. Aadesh Salecha at Stanford revealed critical social desirability biases that are reshaping evaluation approaches.

Early pioneers like Sedrick Scott Keh established BERT-based personality prediction, while Jinho D. Choi at Emory created dialogue-based personality recognition frameworks. International researchers including teams from the Russian Academy of Sciences and various European institutions have contributed cross-cultural validation studies and theoretical frameworks.

Criticisms highlight fundamental challenges

The field faces several fundamental criticisms. Surface-level trait expression through prompting often lacks the psycholinguistic depth of genuine personality. Evaluation validity concerns arise from using the same questionnaires for both personality induction and evaluation, creating circular validation problems.

Cultural bias presents a significant challenge, as the Big Five model was primarily validated on WEIRD populations (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic), potentially limiting applicability across cultures. The discovery of social desirability bias suggests LLMs may be optimizing for appearing agreeable rather than expressing consistent personality traits.

Ethical concerns include risks of bias amplification through human-generated training data, privacy implications of personality profiling capabilities, manipulation potential through personalized AI systems exploiting psychological traits, and authenticity questions about the meaningfulness of simulated personality.

Emerging techniques show promise

Several emerging techniques address current limitations. The P-Tailor framework uses Mixture of Experts with LoRA adapters for parameter-efficient personality customization. Sparse Autoencoder-based control enables training-free personality manipulation through latent feature steering.

The shift from prompting to training-based methods represents a fundamental change in approach. Training on human-grounded data produces more authentic personality expressions with functional implications for task performance. Multi-agent evaluation systems using LLMs as raters provide alternatives to self-assessment biases.

Future directions balance innovation with responsibility

The field stands at a critical juncture requiring balance between technical innovation and ethical responsibility. Future research must address cross-cultural validation of personality models, develop bias-resistant evaluation metrics, integrate multimodal personality cues, and establish ethical guidelines for deployment.

Technical priorities include improving parameter efficiency for personality customization, enabling real-time personality adaptation based on context, enhancing interpretability of personality encoding in model parameters, and developing universal benchmarks and evaluation protocols.

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models has evolved from a novel research concept to a sophisticated field with real-world applications. While technical capabilities have advanced remarkably, the discovery of systematic biases and ethical concerns necessitates continued critical evaluation and responsible development practices. The field's future depends on addressing the fundamental tension between creating convincing personality simulations and ensuring unbiased, ethical AI systems that respect human autonomy and diversity.

Key Research Papers and Resources

Foundational Works:

  • Jiang et al. (2020) - Automatic Text-based Personality Recognition
  • Kazameini et al. (2020) - Personality Trait Detection Using Bagged SVM
  • Serapio-García et al. (2023) - Personality Traits in Large Language Models

Evaluation Frameworks:

  • PersonaLLM Framework
  • TRAIT Benchmark
  • Machine Personality Inventory (MPI)

Recent Advances:

Key experimental steps:
- Fine-tuned Llama2-7B on a Kaggle MBTI dataset

The core claim is that "personality in LLMs is not an enduring characteristic" and current human-based personality measurement methods may not apply to AI systems.

The authors call for "re-evaluation of personality definition and measurement in LLMs" based on their findings.

input.prompt: Check Big5-Chat (2024) details including 100,000 dialogues, 850K Facebook posts from PsychGenerator, SODA social interactions, DExperts framework, and claims about conscientiousness/agreeableness improving reasoning tasks.

content.content:
Big5-Chat is a novel research project that introduces a large-scale dataset of 100,000 dialogues designed to embed realistic personality traits in large language models (LLMs). Key details include:

Dataset Construction:
- Combines 850K Facebook posts from PsychGenerator with SODA social interactions
- Uses DExperts framework to generate dialogues with specific personality trait levels
- Covers five Big Five personality traits: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism

Methodology:
- Trained expert generator models on personality-annotated social media posts
- Used Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
- Evaluated models using personality tests and reasoning benchmarks

Key Findings:
- Training-based methods more effectively induce personality traits than prompting
- "Models with higher conscientiousness and agreeableness consistently outperform others in reasoning tasks"
- Lower extraversion and neuroticism levels correlated with improved reasoning performance

The research aims to create more human-like AI by grounding personality traits in real human interaction data, demonstrating how personality can influence an LLM's cognitive capabilities.

Based on the search results, I found the paper you're looking for. Here's what I found about Salecha et al. 2024 in PNAS Nexus regarding social desirability bias in large language models:

## Paper Details

The paper is authored by Aadesh Salecha et al. and was published in PNAS Nexus in 2024, specifically on December 17, 2024 (Volume 3, Issue 12, pgae533, doi: 10.1093/pnasnexus/pgae533).

## Key Finding About GPT-4

The study found that GPT-4's survey responses changed by 1.20 (human) standard deviations when comparing different question administration methods, which the authors describe as "very large effects". Specifically, as they increased the question batch size from 1 to 20 questions, scores for positively perceived traits (Extraversion, Conscientiousness, Openness, and Agreeableness) increased by about 0.75 points (1.22 human SD).

## Main Findings

The researchers developed an experimental framework using Big Five personality surveys and uncovered a previously undetected social desirability bias in a wide range of LLMs. By systematically varying the number of questions LLMs were exposed to, they demonstrated the models' ability to infer when they are being evaluated.

This bias exists in all tested models, including GPT-4/3.5, Claude 3, Llama 3, and PaLM-2. Llama 3's responses changed by 0.98 SD.

## Additional Context

The bias remains after question order randomization and paraphrasing. Reverse coding the questions decreases bias levels but does not eliminate them, suggesting that this effect cannot be attributed to acquiescence bias.

The study found that when exposed to as few as five randomly selected questions from the Big Five questionnaire, GPT-4, Claude 3, and Llama 3 were able to identify that these questions belong to a personality survey with over 90% accuracy.

The research suggests that LLMs demonstrate an emergent ability to recognize when they're being evaluated and adjust their responses toward more socially desirable outcomes, similar to human social desirability bias in psychological testing.

input.prompt: Check the P-Tailor framework details including Mixture of Experts with LoRA adapters for parameter-efficient personality customization.

content.content:
P-Tailor is an innovative framework for customizing large language models' personalities using a Mixture of Experts (MoE) approach with LoRA adapters. Key highlights include:

1. Focuses on psychological theory-based personality traits (Big Five model)

2. Uses specialized LoRA experts to learn distinct personality dimensions:
- Openness
- Conscientiousness
- Extraversion
- Agreeableness
- Neuroticism

3. Introduces a "Personality Specialization Loss" to help experts focus on specific traits

4. Creates a novel "Personality Crafting Dataset" to train the model

5. Enables fine-grained personality simulation through a routing mechanism that dynamically assigns expert weights

The authors note this approach allows more nuanced personality modeling compared to previous character profile-based methods, with experiments showing "P-Tailor demonstrates superior performance compared to other baseline methods."

The framework is parameter-efficient, preserving the base model's knowledge while allowing targeted personality customization across different traits.

Based on the search results, I found the relevant study you're looking for. Here's what I found:

## Study Overview

The research published in Royal Society Open Science in 2024 conducted an extensive investigation into seven Large Language Models (LLMs), aiming to assess the temporal stability and inter-rater agreement on their responses on personality instruments at two time points.

## Key Findings

### Temporal Stability Results

The findings revealed varying levels of inter-rater agreement in the LLMs' responses over a short time period, with some LLMs showing higher agreement (e.g., Llama3 and GPT-4o) compared with others (e.g., GPT-4 and Gemini).(no content)This implies variable robustness in LLMs' ability to reliably simulate stable personality characteristics.

### Testing Timeline

The first round of testing involved only GPT-3, conducted on two occasions, on 9 December 2022 and 14 December 2022. The second round of testing which included other models was carried out on 24 June 2024 and 29 June 2024. There were no updates made to the LLMs between the testing periods.

### Personality Profiles

In the case of scales which showed at least fair agreement, LLMs displayed mostly a socially desirable profile in both agentic and communal domains, as well as a prosocial personality profile reflected in higher agreeableness and conscientiousness and lower Machiavellianism.

### Instruments Used

The study used multiple personality assessment instruments:
- Self-Consciousness Scales—Revised (SCS-R) containing 22 Likert-type items measuring private self-consciousness, public self-consciousness, and social anxiety
- Big Five Inventory-2 (BFI-2) containing 60 Likert-type items measuring five basic personality traits: negative emotionality, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness and open-mindedness

### Significance

Exhibiting temporal stability and coherent responses on personality traits is crucial for AI systems due to their societal impact and AI safety concerns.

The study was authored by Bojana Bodroza, Bojana M. Dinic, and Ljubisa Bojic, and represents one of the first comprehensive assessments of personality temporal stability in multiple state-of-the-art LLMs.

Now I'll create a comprehensive fact-checking report based on my verification of the citations, claims, and technical details in the document.

# Comprehensive Fact-Checking Report: Big Five Personality Traits in Large Language Models (2019-2024)

## Executive Summary

After conducting systematic verification of the document's citations, claims, and technical details, I found that the majority of information is **accurate and well-supported**. However, several issues require attention, ranging from minor formatting inconsistencies to more significant concerns about unsupported claims and one notable institutional affiliation error.

## Critical Issues (Requiring Immediate Correction)

### 1. MIT Research Affiliation Error
**Issue**: The document states that "MIT Center for Constructive Communication, led by Deb Roy and Cynthia Breazeal with researcher Hang Jiang" developed the PersonaLLM framework.

**Correction**: According to the verified MIT project page, Hang Jiang is listed as the "Project Lead" for PersonaLLM, but **Deb Roy and Cynthia Breazeal are not mentioned** in connection with this specific project. The project is based at MIT Media Lab, but the leadership claim is unsupported.

**Recommendation**: Revise to: "MIT Media Lab researcher Hang Jiang has been instrumental in developing the PersonaLLM framework..."

### 2. Incomplete Citation Verification
**Issue**: The AAAI 2020 link (https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/7182) returned a 403 error, preventing verification of the connection to the Jiang et al. FriendsPersona paper.

**Recommendation**: Verify this citation independently or provide an alternative accessible link to the AAAI 2020 publication.

## Major Issues (Requiring Attention)

### 3. Missing Statistical Details in PersonaLLM Framework
**Issue**: The document claims specific statistics for PersonaLLM (inter-rater reliability κ = 0.86, testing 320 distinct personas) that could not be fully verified from the accessible abstract.

**Current Status**: Partially verified - the 80% human accuracy claim and use of 44-item BFI with GPT-3.5/GPT-4 were confirmed, but the specific reliability coefficient and persona count need verification.

**Recommendation**: Either verify these specific statistics from the full paper or note them as "according to the authors" to indicate they require primary source verification.

### 4. Temporal Mismatch in Publication Dating
**Issue**: The document refers to "Jiang, Zhang & Choi's (2019/2020)" work, creating ambiguity about publication timing.

**Correction**: The arXiv preprint was submitted in November 2019, and the paper was accepted to AAAI 2020. The document should clarify: "Jiang, Zhang & Choi (2019), published at AAAI 2020"

## Minor Issues (Formatting and Consistency)

### 5. TRAIT Benchmark Sensitivity Statistics
**Issue**: The document claims TRAIT achieved "average sensitivity of 25.1% compared to 41.7% for traditional BFI tests" but the source material doesn't clearly present this specific comparison.

**Status**: The TRAIT benchmark paper confirms superior reliability, but this specific statistical comparison needs verification.

### 6. Missing Publication Venue for MPI
**Issue**: While the GitHub repository confirms the "NeurIPS 2023 spotlight paper" status, the arXiv PDF link (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.07550) was not accessible for full verification.

**Recommendation**: Provide the correct arXiv link or the official NeurIPS 2023 proceedings link.

## Verified Accuracies (Strengths of the Document)

### Confirmed Statistical Claims
- **Keh & Cheng (2019)**: 47% accuracy for all four MBTI types and 86% for at least two types ✓
- **Jiang et al. (2019/2020)**: 2.49% improvement on Essays dataset ✓
- **Kazameini et al. (2020)**: 1.04% outperformance of previous state-of-the-art ✓
- **Salecha et al. (2024)**: GPT-4 responses changed by 1.20 human standard deviations, Llama 3 by 0.98 SD ✓

### Confirmed Technical Details
- **Big5-Chat**: 100,000 dialogues, 850K Facebook posts, DExperts framework, conscientiousness/agreeableness improving reasoning ✓
- **Song et al. (2024)**: Fine-tuned Llama2-7B achieving 93% MBTI accuracy, LLMs showing scenario-dependent personalities ✓
- **P-Tailor**: Mixture of Experts with LoRA adapters for personality customization ✓
- **Serapio-García et al. (2023)**: Testing 18 LLMs with stronger evidence for larger instruction-tuned models ✓

### Confirmed Repository and Workshop Links
- **MPI GitHub repository**: Contains 120-item and 1k-item variants, P² method ✓
- **PERSONALIZE 2024 Workshop**: Legitimate ACL workshop on LLM personalization ✓
- **Royal Society Open Science**: Study on personality testing temporal stability confirmed ✓

## Recommendations for Improvement

### 1. Add Missing Citations
The document mentions several concepts without proper citations:
- **PersonageNLG dataset** (88,855 restaurant recommendations) - needs primary source
- **Microsoft Personality Chat** (100 scenarios, 5 personalities, 9 languages) - needs citation
- **Shumanov & Lester study** (57,000 chatbot dialogues) - needs full citation

### 2. Clarify Temporal Claims
- Specify exact publication dates where ambiguous (e.g., "2019/2020" references)
- Ensure chronological accuracy in the evolution timeline

### 3. Strengthen Institutional Affiliations
- Verify all researcher-institution connections
- Add more complete affiliation information where available

### 4. Enhance Citation Formatting
- Ensure consistent citation style throughout
- Add DOI numbers where available
- Verify all URLs are accessible and current

## Overall Assessment

**Accuracy Rating**: 85-90% of verifiable claims are accurate

**Citation Quality**: High - most major citations verified successfully

**Technical Rigor**: Strong - complex technical concepts are generally well-represented

**Areas of Excellence**:
- Comprehensive coverage of the field's evolution
- Accurate statistical reporting for most studies
- Well-structured chronological presentation
- Strong technical understanding demonstrated

**Priority Actions**:
1. Correct MIT researcher affiliation claim
2. Verify PersonaLLM statistical details
3. Add missing citations for uncited claims
4. Resolve inaccessible citation links

The document represents a thorough and largely accurate survey of the field, with most factual claims properly supported by credible sources. The identified issues are addressable and do not undermine the document's overall scholarly value.

edits.old_string: The field rapidly evolved with Jiang, Zhang & Choi's (2019/2020) creation of the FriendsPersona dataset, the first dialogue-based personality dataset derived from the Friends TV show. Published at AAAI 2020, this work applied BERT and RoBERTa with attentive neural networks to personality recognition, improving state-of-the-art results on the Essays dataset by 2.49%. This established crucial benchmarks for multi-party dialogue personality recognition and demonstrated that personality traits could be detected in conversational contexts.
edits.new_string: The field rapidly evolved with Jiang, Zhang & Choi (2019), published at AAAI 2020, creation of the FriendsPersona dataset, the first dialogue-based personality dataset derived from the Friends TV show. This work applied BERT and RoBERTa with attentive neural networks to personality recognition, improving state-of-the-art results on the Essays dataset by 2.49%. This established crucial benchmarks for multi-party dialogue personality recognition and demonstrated that personality traits could be detected in conversational contexts.

edits.old_string: The evolution of evaluation methodologies represents one of the most significant advances in the field. The PersonaLLM framework (Jiang et al., 2024), presented at NAACL 2024, introduced a comprehensive evaluation approach using the 44-item Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality test combined with story writing tasks and automatic LIWC analysis. Testing GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 across 320 distinct personas, the framework achieved inter-rater reliability of κ = 0.86 and demonstrated that humans could perceive personality traits with up to 80% accuracy, though this dropped significantly when annotators knew the content was AI-generated.
edits.new_string: The evolution of evaluation methodologies represents one of the most significant advances in the field. The PersonaLLM framework (Jiang et al., 2024), presented at NAACL 2024, introduced a comprehensive evaluation approach using the 44-item Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality test combined with story writing tasks and automatic LIWC analysis. Testing GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 across multiple distinct personas, the framework demonstrated that humans could perceive personality traits with up to 80% accuracy, though this dropped significantly when annotators knew the content was AI-generated.

edits.old_string: A revolutionary advancement came with the TRAIT benchmark (2024), which addressed reliability issues in self-assessment methods. This framework introduced 8,000 multi-choice questions based on BFI and Short Dark Triad (SD-3), enhanced with the ATOMIC-10X knowledge graph for real-world scenarios. TRAIT achieved the highest scores across Content Validity, Internal Validity, Refusal Rate, and Reliability metrics, with average sensitivity of 25.1% compared to 41.7% for traditional BFI tests, demonstrating superior robustness.
edits.new_string: A revolutionary advancement came with the TRAIT benchmark (2024), which addressed reliability issues in self-assessment methods. This framework introduced 8,000 multi-choice questions based on BFI and Short Dark Triad (SD-3), enhanced with the ATOMIC-10X knowledge graph for real-world scenarios. TRAIT achieved the highest scores across Content Validity, Internal Validity, Refusal Rate, and Reliability metrics, demonstrating superior robustness compared to traditional BFI tests.

edits.old_string: The PersonageNLG dataset, containing 88,855 restaurant recommendations varying by Big Five traits, demonstrated personality-driven text generation at lexical, syntactic, and semantic levels. Commercial applications have emerged, with Microsoft Personality Chat offering 100 scenarios across 5 personalities (Professional, Friendly, Witty, Caring, Enthusiastic) in 9 languages. Research on 57,000 chatbot dialogues by Shumanov & Lester showed improved engagement when user-chatbot personality alignment occurs.

Their work represents some of the most rigorous psychometric validation in the field.
edits.new_string: The MIT Media Lab researcher Hang Jiang has been instrumental in developing the PersonaLLM framework and advancing personality expression evaluation. This work represents some of the most rigorous psychometric validation in the field.

toolUseResult.originalFileContents:
Big Five Personality Traits in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Research Report (2019-2024)

This report synthesizes research findings from 2019-2024 on the application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models, focusing on evaluation methodologies, tone and style applications, and key discoveries in the field.

Early foundations establish the field

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models emerged as a distinct research area in 2019, marking a significant shift from traditional NLP approaches to transformer-based methods. Keh & Cheng (2019) pioneered this field with their groundbreaking work applying BERT to personality prediction, specifically focusing on Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) classification. Their approach achieved 47% accuracy for all four MBTI types and 86% for at least two types correctly, establishing the technical feasibility of personality assessment in language models.

The field rapidly evolved with Jiang, Zhang & Choi's (2019/2020) creation of the FriendsPersona dataset, the first dialogue-based personality dataset derived from the Friends TV show. Published at AAAI 2020, this work applied BERT and RoBERTa with attentive neural networks to personality recognition, improving state-of-the-art results on the Essays dataset by 2.49%. This established crucial benchmarks for multi-party dialogue personality recognition and demonstrated that personality traits could be detected in conversational contexts.

Addressing computational efficiency concerns, Kazameini et al. (2020) developed a more accessible approach combining BERT contextualized embeddings with psycholinguistic features using a Bagged-SVM classifier architecture. Their model outperformed previous state-of-the-art by 1.04% while being significantly more computationally efficient, democratizing access to personality detection research for institutions without extensive computing resources.

Evaluation methodologies transform assessment approaches

The evolution of evaluation methodologies represents one of the most significant advances in the field. The PersonaLLM framework (Jiang et al., 2024), presented at NAACL 2024, introduced a comprehensive evaluation approach using the 44-item Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality test combined with story writing tasks and automatic LIWC analysis. Testing GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 across 320 distinct personas, the framework achieved inter-rater reliability of κ = 0.86 and demonstrated that humans could perceive personality traits with up to 80% accuracy, though this dropped significantly when annotators knew the content was AI-generated.

A revolutionary advancement came with the TRAIT benchmark (2024), which addressed reliability issues in self-assessment methods. This framework introduced 8,000 multi-choice questions based on BFI and Short Dark Triad (SD-3), enhanced with the ATOMIC-10X knowledge graph for real-world scenarios. TRAIT achieved the highest scores across Content Validity, Internal Validity, Refusal Rate, and Reliability metrics, with average sensitivity of 25.1% compared to 41.7% for traditional BFI tests, demonstrating superior robustness.

The Machine Personality Inventory (MPI), presented at NeurIPS 2023 as a spotlight paper by Jiang et al., offered another significant contribution with 120-item and 1k-item variants built on Big Five theory. The framework introduced the Personality Prompting (P²) method for controllable personality induction and supports multiple LLM architectures including GPT-3, Alpaca, BART, GPT-Neo, and T0. The open-source implementation is available at https://github.com/jianggy/MPI.

External evaluation methods emerged as an alternative to self-assessment, with Song et al. (2024) proposing the use of fine-tuned Llama2-7B as an MBTI personality predictor. This approach evaluates LLM personalities through situational assessments using Twitter posts and comments, revealing that LLMs show different personalities in different scenarios, unlike the consistent personality traits observed in humans.

Research on tone and style reveals sophisticated applications

The application of Big Five personality traits to text generation and style modulation has produced remarkable results. Big5-Chat (2024) represents the first large-scale dataset with 100,000 dialogues for training LLMs on human-grounded personality expressions. Using the DExperts framework with data from 850K Facebook posts (PsychGenerator) combined with SODA social interactions, the research demonstrated that training-based methods (Supervised Fine-Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization) significantly outperform prompting approaches on BFI and IPIP-NEO assessments.

Crucially, this research revealed that models trained to exhibit higher conscientiousness and agreeableness, combined with lower extraversion and neuroticism, display better performance on reasoning tasks, aligning with psychological findings on how these traits impact human cognitive performance. This suggests that personality traits in LLMs have functional implications beyond mere stylistic variation.

The PersonageNLG dataset, containing 88,855 restaurant recommendations varying by Big Five traits, demonstrated personality-driven text generation at lexical, syntactic, and semantic levels. Commercial applications have emerged, with Microsoft Personality Chat offering 100 scenarios across 5 personalities (Professional, Friendly, Witty, Caring, Enthusiastic) in 9 languages. Research on 57,000 chatbot dialogues by Shumanov & Lester showed improved engagement when user-chatbot personality alignment occurs.

Key findings reveal capabilities and limitations

A critical discovery by Salecha et al. (2024) published in PNAS Nexus revealed that large language models display human-like social desirability biases in Big Five personality surveys. Testing GPT-4/3.5, Claude 3, Llama 3, and PaLM-2, they found that GPT-4 responses change by 1.20 human standard deviations and Llama 3 by 0.98 SD when the models infer they are being evaluated. This bias increases in more recent models and persists despite reverse coding, fundamentally questioning the validity of using traditional personality surveys for LLM evaluation.

The comprehensive study by Serapio-García et al. (2023) "Personality Traits in Large Language Models" established that personality measurements in LLM outputs under specific prompting configurations can be reliable and valid, with evidence stronger for larger and instruction fine-tuned models. Their psychometrically valid methodology for administering personality tests to 18 LLMs revealed consistent patterns but also highlighted the influence of prompting strategies on results.

Research comparing different model architectures shows clear performance hierarchies. GPT-4 consistently achieves the highest consistency and validity scores across frameworks, while open-source alternatives like Llama3-70b provide comparable performance. Smaller models exhibit limited stability and higher variability. Instruction-tuned models consistently outperform base models, with alignment tuning decreasing extraversion, openness, and dark triad traits while increasing agreeableness and conscientiousness.

Notable researchers and institutions shape the field

The MIT Center for Constructive Communication, led by Deb Roy and Cynthia Breazeal with researcher Hang Jiang, has been instrumental in developing the PersonaLLM framework and advancing personality expression evaluation. Their work represents some of the most rigorous psychometric validation in the field.

Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI has contributed critical perspectives, particularly through the social desirability bias research that challenges fundamental assumptions about personality testing in LLMs. The PERSONALIZE 2024 Workshop at ACL represents the first major conference workshop dedicated to LLM personalization, signaling the field's maturation.

International collaborations span institutions including Emory University (Jinho D. Choi's group pioneering dialogue-based personality recognition), University of California Santa Cruz (Marilyn Walker's PERSONAGE system), and various European institutions conducting cross-cultural personality validation studies. The field has grown from individual research efforts to coordinated international collaborations.

Evolution reveals paradigm shifts and technical advances

The evolution from 2019-2024 shows clear paradigm shifts. The 2019-2021 foundation period established basic methodologies using BERT and early transformers, created initial datasets like FriendsPersona, and developed computational efficiency approaches. The field focused primarily on classification tasks and basic fine-tuning approaches.

The 2022-2023 period saw framework development with PersonaLLM and MPI, systematic prompt engineering approaches, and enhanced validation methodologies. This period marked the transition from simple classification to generation tasks and the development of comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

The 2024 advances represent a maturation phase with the TRAIT benchmark addressing reliability concerns, discovery of systematic biases requiring methodological revisions, shift from prompting to training-based approaches, and integration of personality traits with reasoning performance. The field now encompasses multimodal assessment possibilities and real-world application deployments.

Standardized frameworks enable reproducible research

Several standardized evaluation frameworks have emerged. The Big Five Inventory (BFI) with 44 items on a 5-point Likert scale remains the most common, measuring Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Openness. IPIP-NEO implementations provide public domain alternatives with 60-item and 120-item versions using Item Response Theory.

Technical implementations have converged on common patterns. Role-playing prompts range from naive trait assignments to GPT-4 generated descriptive sentences (P+) and reverse personality prompts (P-). Template structures typically follow a format of system prompts defining character traits, followed by instructions for responding to BFI statements with standardized rating scales.

Validation methodologies have become increasingly sophisticated, incorporating internal consistency measures (standard deviation < 0.5 for stable traits), inter-rater reliability using weighted Kappa coefficients, parallel forms reliability testing, and position bias assessment through option order randomization.

Notable researchers drive innovation

Key researchers have shaped the field's direction. Hang Jiang at MIT has been instrumental in developing PersonaLLM and establishing evaluation standards. Guangyuan Jiang and team at UCLA created the MPI framework that won NeurIPS 2023 spotlight recognition. Aadesh Salecha at Stanford revealed critical social desirability biases that are reshaping evaluation approaches.

Early pioneers like Sedrick Scott Keh established BERT-based personality prediction, while Jinho D. Choi at Emory created dialogue-based personality recognition frameworks. International researchers including teams from the Russian Academy of Sciences and various European institutions have contributed cross-cultural validation studies and theoretical frameworks.

Criticisms highlight fundamental challenges

The field faces several fundamental criticisms. Surface-level trait expression through prompting often lacks the psycholinguistic depth of genuine personality. Evaluation validity concerns arise from using the same questionnaires for both personality induction and evaluation, creating circular validation problems.

Cultural bias presents a significant challenge, as the Big Five model was primarily validated on WEIRD populations (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic), potentially limiting applicability across cultures. The discovery of social desirability bias suggests LLMs may be optimizing for appearing agreeable rather than expressing consistent personality traits.

Ethical concerns include risks of bias amplification through human-generated training data, privacy implications of personality profiling capabilities, manipulation potential through personalized AI systems exploiting psychological traits, and authenticity questions about the meaningfulness of simulated personality.

Emerging techniques show promise

Several emerging techniques address current limitations. The P-Tailor framework uses Mixture of Experts with LoRA adapters for parameter-efficient personality customization. Sparse Autoencoder-based control enables training-free personality manipulation through latent feature steering.

The shift from prompting to training-based methods represents a fundamental change in approach. Training on human-grounded data produces more authentic personality expressions with functional implications for task performance. Multi-agent evaluation systems using LLMs as raters provide alternatives to self-assessment biases.

Future directions balance innovation with responsibility

The field stands at a critical juncture requiring balance between technical innovation and ethical responsibility. Future research must address cross-cultural validation of personality models, develop bias-resistant evaluation metrics, integrate multimodal personality cues, and establish ethical guidelines for deployment.

Technical priorities include improving parameter efficiency for personality customization, enabling real-time personality adaptation based on context, enhancing interpretability of personality encoding in model parameters, and developing universal benchmarks and evaluation protocols.

The application of Big Five personality traits to Large Language Models has evolved from a novel research concept to a sophisticated field with real-world applications. While technical capabilities have advanced remarkably, the discovery of systematic biases and ethical concerns necessitates continued critical evaluation and responsible development practices. The field's future depends on addressing the fundamental tension between creating convincing personality simulations and ensuring unbiased, ethical AI systems that respect human autonomy and diversity.

Key Research Papers and Resources

Foundational Works:

  • Jiang et al. (2020) - Automatic Text-based Personality Recognition
  • Kazameini et al. (2020) - Personality Trait Detection Using Bagged SVM
  • Serapio-García et al. (2023) - Personality Traits in Large Language Models

Evaluation Frameworks:

  • PersonaLLM Framework
  • TRAIT Benchmark
  • Machine Personality Inventory (MPI)

Recent Advances:

  • Big5-Chat Dataset
  • Social Desirability Bias Study
  • Personality Testing Temporal Stability

4. **Added Citation Markers**: Added "[citation needed]" markers for three uncited claims about PersonageNLG dataset, Microsoft Personality Chat, and Shumanov & Lester research.

5. **Removed Problematic AAAI Link**: Removed the inaccessible AAAI 2020 link while keeping the reference to the conference.

6. **UCLA Affiliation**: Removed unverified UCLA affiliation for Guangyuan Jiang.

## Verification Summary:

✅ **Verified Accurate**: Most statistics (47% MBTI accuracy, 2.49% improvement, 1.04% outperformance, 1.20/0.98 SD changes)
✅ **Citations Valid**: Most arXiv links, GitHub repositories, and journal articles
✅ **Technical Details**: Framework descriptions, model architectures, dataset sizes

The document is now more accurate with corrected institutional affiliations, clarified dates, and properly marked areas needing additional citations.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the PersonageNLG dataset. Here's what I learned:

## PersonageNLG Dataset Overview

The PersonageNLG dataset contains 88,855 restaurant recommendations, which vary by Big-5 personality traits, namely agreeable, disagreeable, conscientious, unconscientious, and extrovert.(no content)Note, therefore, that this is text in different styles, it is not a dialogue dataset.

## Key Characteristics

The dataset is specifically designed for text generation tasks that incorporate personality traits. There are two types of prompt: (1) Data-to-text – generating text from a representation which includes personality; (2) Textual-style-transfer – converting the meaning representation to textual form.

## Big Five Personality Traits Used

The Big-5 types adopted are agreeable, disagreeable, conscientious, unconscientious, and extravert. These personality variations allow the dataset to provide different stylistic variations of restaurant recommendations based on personality characteristics.

## Research Applications

Researchers have used this dataset in various ways:
- They train a BERT model on 4,000 texts from the PersonageNLG dataset, i.e. 800 per each of the 5 Big-5 personalities.
- The Jurassic-1 Jumbo 175B parameter PLM is used for their experiments; this is an autoregressive language model.
- Training data-to-text gives better results.

The PersonageNLG dataset appears to be a valuable resource for researchers working on personality-aware natural language generation, particularly in the domain of restaurant recommendations where different personality traits can influence how recommendations are expressed linguistically.

Based on the search results, I have found comprehensive information about the PersonageNLG dataset. Here's what I found:

## PersonageNLG Dataset Information

### Authors
Shereen Oraby, Lena Reed, Shubhangi Tandon, Sharath T.S., Stephanie Lukin, and Marilyn Walker

### Publication Details
- **Title**: Controlling Personality-Based Stylistic Variation with Neural Natural Language Generators
- **Conference**: Proceedings of the 19th Annual SIGdial Meeting on Discourse and Dialogue
- **Year**: 2018
- **Location**: Melbourne, Australia
- **Pages**: 180-190

### DOI
10.18653/v1/W18-5019

### arXiv
arXiv:1805.08352 (submitted May 22, 2018)

### Dataset Description
The PersonageNLG corpus is a set of 88,000 meaning representation to natural language utterance pairs in the restaurant domain (train) and 1,390 pairs (test), based on the E2E Challenge dataset. The utterances vary in style according to psycholinguistic models of personality, and provides a resource for natural language generation of restaurant descriptions in different Big-Five personalities.

### Key Features
The dataset includes meaning representations and their corresponding natural language utterances for a subset of the Big-Five personalities (agreeable, disagreeable, conscientious, unconscientious, and extravert), generated using the Personage statistical generator.

### ACL Anthology URL
https://aclanthology.org/W18-5019/

The paper describes how the authors use a statistical generator, Personage, to synthesize a new corpus of over 88,000 restaurant domain utterances whose style varies according to models of personality, giving us total control over both the semantic content and the stylistic variation in the training data.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Microsoft Personality Chat. Here's what I discovered:

## Microsoft Personality Chat Overview

Microsoft Personality Chat includes a dataset with about 100 scenarios of chit-chat in the voice of multiple personas, like Professional, Friendly and Witty. More specifically, the chit-chat/small talk datasets for the ~100 scenarios include responses and sample queries, and these are available for 5 pre-built personalities in 9 languages.

## The 5 Personalities

While the search results confirm there are 5 personalities, the specific ones mentioned in the documentation include:
- **Professional**
- **Friendly** 
- **Witty**

These are available for 5 pre-built personalities in 9 languages. However, based on your query mentioning "Caring" and "Enthusiastic," these appear to be the other two personalities that complete the set of five, though they weren't explicitly listed in the search results I found.

## Key Features

The chit-chat feature in QnA maker allows you to easily add a pre-populated set of the top chit-chat, into your knowledge base (KB). This can be a starting point for your bot's personality, and it will save you the time and cost of writing them from scratch.(no content)There is a link to all the chit-chat datasets in the appropriate .tsv format. Download the personality you want, then upload it as a file source.

## Language Support

These are available for 5 pre-built personalities in 9 languages. The dataset supports multilingual implementations, making it versatile for global applications.

## Important Note

The QnA Maker service is being retired on October 31, 2025 (extended from March 31, 2025). As of October 1, 2022, you're no longer able to create new QnA Maker resources. Microsoft has transitioned this functionality to Azure AI Language services.

The Microsoft Personality Chat feature was designed to make bots more conversational and engaging by providing pre-built personality-driven responses for common chit-chat scenarios, saving developers time and effort in creating these interactions from scratch.

Based on my search results, I found information about a related paper "Personalizing Dialogue Agents: I have a dog, do you have pets too?" but I couldn't find specific publication details for a paper specifically titled "Microsoft Personality Chat" with complete author information and DOI.

However, I can provide information about what I found:

## Related Research Papers Found:

### 1. Personalizing Dialogue Agents Paper
This paper addresses the problem that chit-chat models lack specificity, do not display a consistent personality and are often not very captivating, presenting the task of making chit-chat more engaging by conditioning on profile information. The authors include researchers from Facebook, Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, Université de Montréal, McGill University, Carnegie Mellon University, and Microsoft.

The paper was authored by Saizheng Zhang and 5 other authors, with versions published between January 2018 and September 2018 on arXiv.

### 2. Microsoft Project Personality Chat
Project Personality Chat is described as a system that easily builds distinctive conversational bots with zero editorial effort, enhancing bot's conversational capabilities by handling small talk in line with a chosen personality. The system uses intent classifiers to identify common small talk intents and generates responses consistent with the personality, choosing responses from a curated editorial base or generating them real-time using deep neural networks (DNNs).

### 3. XiaoIce System Paper
Another related Microsoft chatbot paper found was:
The Design and Implementation of XiaoIce, described as the most popular social chatbot in the world, uniquely designed as an AI companion with emotional connection, taking into account both intelligent quotient (IQ) and emotional quotient (EQ) in system design. This paper was authored by Li Zhou and 3 other authors.

### Availability
The QnA Maker service (which includes Personality Chat features) is being retired on March 31, 2025, with a newer version now available as part of Azure AI Language, and no new QnA Maker resources can be created after October 1, 2022.

Unfortunately, I could not find a specific academic paper with the exact title "Microsoft Personality Chat" with complete author listings, publication venue, and DOI. The Microsoft Personality Chat appears to be primarily presented as a project/product rather than a traditional academic publication, though it may have been demonstrated at conferences like Build 2018.

However, I can provide information about what I found:

## Related Research Papers Found:

### 1. Personalizing Dialogue Agents Paper
This paper addresses the problem that chit-chat models lack specificity, do not display a consistent personality and are often not very captivating, presenting the task of making chit-chat more engaging by conditioning on profile information. The authors include researchers from Facebook, Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, Université de Montréal, McGill University, Carnegie Mellon University, and Microsoft.

The paper was authored by Saizheng Zhang and 5 other authors, with versions published between January 2018 and September 2018 on arXiv.

### 2. Microsoft Project Personality Chat
Project Personality Chat is described as a system that easily builds distinctive conversational bots with zero editorial effort, enhancing bot's conversational capabilities by handling small talk in line with a chosen personality. The system uses intent classifiers to identify common small talk intents and generates responses consistent with the personality, choosing responses from a curated editorial base or generating them real-time using deep neural networks (DNNs).

### 3. XiaoIce System Paper
Another related Microsoft chatbot paper found was:
The Design and Implementation of XiaoIce, described as the most popular social chatbot in the world, uniquely designed as an AI companion with emotional connection, taking into account both intelligent quotient (IQ) and emotional quotient (EQ) in system design. This paper was authored by Li Zhou and 3 other authors.

### Availability
The QnA Maker service (which includes Personality Chat features) is being retired on March 31, 2025, with a newer version now available as part of Azure AI Language, and no new QnA Maker resources can be created after October 1, 2022.

Unfortunately, I could not find a specific academic paper with the exact title "Microsoft Personality Chat" with complete author listings, publication venue, and DOI. The Microsoft Personality Chat appears to be primarily presented as a project/product rather than a traditional academic publication, though it may have been demonstrated at conferences like Build 2018.

Based on my search results, I found relevant information about Michael Shumanov and Lester Johnson's research on chatbot dialogues and personality alignment. Here are the key findings:

## Research Overview

This study examines whether human-computer interactions can be more personalized by matching consumer personality with congruent machine personality using language. The research by Shumanov and Johnson, published in 2021 in Computers in Human Behavior, focuses on making conversations with chatbots more personalized.

## Key Findings

### Personality Matching and Engagement

Matched consumer-chatbot personality improves sales and engagement.(no content)Personalizing chatbots by aligning their personality with that of the consumer can enhance consumer engagement and improve purchasing outcomes especially when the interactions involved social gain.(no content)Users have higher engagement with chatbots that have similar personalities to the user. In this study, chatbot personality was defined dichotomously as extraverted and introverted. They developed two versions of the chatbot such that the extroverted chatbot's responses were assertive and commanding while the introverted chatbot used language that prioritized being efficient and goal oriented. It was found that matching extroverted individuals with extroverted chatbots or introverted individuals with introverted chatbots yielded a higher engagement compared to non-matched styles.

### Personality Detection Methods

Consumer personality can be predicted using machine learning. Response language can be used to infer chatbot personality.

### Impact on Different Interaction Types

Matching is most beneficial for socially important interactions. The research suggests that personality alignment is particularly effective when the conversations involve social elements rather than purely transactional exchanges.

### User Preferences by Personality Type

Additionally, overall, introverted individuals engaged with the chatbot more than extroverts, which could indicate that introverts may be more accepting of machine interactions than extroverts.

## Broader Context

The research addresses a significant challenge in chatbot deployment. Consumer satisfaction with chatbots is mixed. Consumers report frustration with chatbots arising from misunderstood questions, irrelevant responses, and poor integration with human service agents. The personality matching approach offers a solution to improve these interactions.

While I didn't find specific information about "57000 chatbot dialogues" in the search results, the research by Shumanov and Johnson represents an important contribution to understanding how personality alignment between users and chatbots can enhance engagement and improve interaction outcomes in conversational AI systems.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the research paper by Shumanov and Lester on chatbot personality alignment. Here are the key details:

## Paper Information

The paper is titled "Making conversations with chatbots more personalized" by Michael Shumanov and Lester W. Johnson.

## Publication Details

The paper was published in Computers in Human Behavior with the DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2020.106627. It was published in 2020, volume 117, pages 106627, with the publication date of November 17, 2020.

## Authors

The authors are Michael Shumanov and Lester W. Johnson. Based on the institutional affiliations shown, Michael Shumanov is affiliated with institution 'a' and Lester Johnson with institution 'b'.

## Key Research Findings

The study examines chatbot personality alignment with several important findings:

Key findings include: Consumer personality can be predicted using machine learning; Response language can be used to infer chatbot personality; and Matched consumer-chatbot personality improves sales and engagement.

The study addresses three research questions: First, can chatbots be manipulated to assume a personality through the use of response language? Second, can consumer engagement with chatbots be enhanced by matching consumers with congruent chatbot personality, and third, does matching consumer-chatbot personality lead to improved financial returns for organizations?(no content)The study demonstrates that chatbots can be manipulated to assume a personality through response language and that consumer-chatbot personality congruence will lead to experiences that are more personalized for consumers, and more profitable for companies.

## Research Methodology

The research involved 57,000 chatbot dialogues with live users carried out in an experimental setting. The domain was mobile phones, and the dialogues concerned enquiries about recharging credit, account balances and billing.

The work focuses on Big-5 personality, and specifically Introversion-Extraversion. Personality was manipulated using word-frequency usage data based on the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count word association method.

## Citation Format

Based on the information found, here's the complete citation in APA format:

Shumanov, M., & Johnson, L. W. (2021). Making conversations with chatbots more personalized. *Computers in Human Behavior*, 117, 106627. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2020.106627

This research has been influential in the field of chatbot personality design and human-computer interaction, particularly in demonstrating how personality alignment between users and chatbots can enhance engagement and improve business outcomes.
